topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the cold finality of a dropped stone. My breath hitched, a sharp, involuntary intake of air that felt too loud in the quiet apartment. I reread the text, the words blurring slightly at the edges. The article—my meticulously crafted piece on urban ecology—was attributed to 'Elias Vance.' My stomach twisted into a tight, nauseating knot. I pressed my palms flat against the desk, the wood grain digging into my skin. How could this have happened? The meticulous cross-referencing, the submission confirmations, the sheer, agonizing effort of writing it all... it was all erased, overwritten by a stranger’s identity. A tremor started in my hands, making the keyboard feel slippery and alien beneath my fingertips. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small space, the silence of the room amplifying the frantic drumming in my chest.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, my vision tunneling. The headline screamed my name, but the byline belonged to someone else entirely. A slow, creeping cold began to spread from my core outward, chilling my extremities. My mouth went dry, and I had to consciously force myself to swallow, the movement feeling clumsy and unnatural. I leaned back in my chair, the springs groaning in protest, and closed my eyes, trying to anchor myself to something real. When I opened them, the light seemed too bright, the room too sharp. A low, ragged sound escaped my throat—a noise somewhere between a gasp and a sob. I picked up the phone, my fingers trembling so violently I nearly dropped it, needing to speak, needing confirmation that this impossible scenario wasn't some bizarre, elaborate prank.

[story 3]

The coffee in my mug went cold before I even registered the temperature drop. I sat motionless at the kitchen table, the printed proof of the publication spread out before me like a damning indictment. Every line was familiar, yet the signature at the bottom was utterly foreign. A wave of disorientation washed over me, making the edges of the room swim. I ran a hand through my hair, feeling the texture rough against my scalp, a desperate attempt to ground myself. My jaw tightened until my teeth ached. I began to methodically review the metadata, clicking through links, searching for any trace of my own input, but the digital landscape offered only smooth, impenetrable walls. I stood up, walking to the window, looking out at the indifferent city, feeling profoundly unbalanced, as if the floor beneath my feet had suddenly tilted.
